The article discusses the process of decomposing visual scenes into three-dimensional bodies using computer vision techniques, focusing on how two-dimensional images captured by devices like film spot scanners can be analyzed and reconstructed into 3D representations. It highlights key concepts such as multi-view geometry, image analysis, and digital image processing within the context of 3D imaging.

The article explores the optimal parameters and resolution limitations of integral photography, presenting a comparative analysis with holography for capturing and displaying 3D information. It derives the ideal lenslet size for lenticular screens and calculates the necessary resolvable spots for effective photographic recording, highlighting the relationship between resolution and reconstruction in this imaging technique.

The article presents a comprehensive framework for a theory of visual pattern recognition in both animals and humans, outlining twelve essential conditions that such a theory must satisfy. It proposes a model consisting of a feature extraction processor, an abstract description mechanism, and a storage system for descriptions, all aimed at explaining various aspects of visual perception and recognition.

The article presents a model for visual luminance discrimination and flicker detection, utilizing RC stages with parametrically controlled time constants to effectively predict psychophysical results related to flicker detection and luminance pulse responses. It highlights the model's ability to account for individual differences and light adaptation effects, drawing parallels between its internal connectivity and synaptic processes in the neural retina.

The article discusses methods for computer recognition of three-dimensional objects within visual scenes, focusing on techniques for scene decomposition, spatial positioning using stereoscopic pairs, background identification, and object isolation despite inaccuracies. It highlights the successful implementation of these methods through computer programs and examples, emphasizing the potential for recognizing objects based solely on monocular geometric properties.

The article discusses a multisurface method for pattern separation in n-dimensional Euclidean space, focusing on the construction of nonlinear manifolds when strict separation of convex hulls is not possible. It highlights the implementation of this method through a computer program, which has been successfully tested on various real-world problems in fields such as image analysis, computer vision, and applied mathematics.

The article explores how individuals recognize and remember faces compared to other familiar objects that are typically viewed from a single orientation, specifically examining the effects of presenting these images upside-down on memory performance. It delves into the implications for visual perception and recognition systems in fields such as computer vision and facial recognition.

The article explores the visual systems in the hamster brain, focusing on how ablation of cortical or tectal areas affects visual localization and discrimination blindness. It also touches on broader themes related to visual modeling, perception, and various applications in visual arts and computing.

The article reviews techniques in computer-based picture processing, focusing on the efficient encoding of images, approximation methods, position-invariant operations, and properties useful for pattern recognition. It also discusses applications such as image restoration, enhancement, and figure extraction, highlighting the importance of these processes in digital imaging and computer vision.

The article "Improved Discrimination in Optical Character Recognition" by H. J. Caulfield and W. T. Maloney discusses advancements in the field of optical character recognition (OCR), focusing on enhanced techniques for image analysis and pattern recognition to improve the accuracy and efficiency of character recognition systems.

The article presents an algorithm for generating continuous skeletons from digitized images of polygonal figures, detailing the analytical simulation of propagation contours and the systematic identification of branch points based on their proximity to the contour. It emphasizes the relationship between computational efficiency and the geometric properties of polygons, contributing to advancements in fields such as computer vision and digital image processing.

The article discusses the advantages of using polygonal curves for data approximation over polynomial methods, particularly when the join points are not predetermined. It presents a dynamic programming approach to efficiently determine an optimal fit for segmented straight lines, addressing the numerical challenges associated with curve fitting.

The article presents a method for determining the shape of a smooth opaque object from a single monocular image, utilizing surface photometry and light-source positioning to resolve ambiguities. It discusses the derivation of a non-linear partial differential equation to analyze intensity at object points, with applications in fields such as lunar topography and scanning electron microscopy, and highlights the implementation of this method using a PDP-6 computer and image-dissector camera.

The article presents a parametric model for a computer-controlled moveable camera on a pan-tilt head, detailing the transformation from object space to image based on control variables. It discusses the development of a calibration system to measure parameters accurately, addresses sources of measurement error, and demonstrates the model's application in stereo vision for object localization and centering in images.

The article investigates the limits and mechanisms of binocular depth discrimination in humans, focusing on how disparity cues affect the perception of depth with varying spatial positions of targets. It highlights the thresholds for reliable depth localization and discusses the implications of these findings in relation to neurophysiology and the functioning of binocular neurons in the visual cortex.

The article discusses the use of diffraction-pattern sampling as a method for automatic pattern recognition in photographic imagery, detailing the generation of diffraction patterns, their relationship to image areas, and the development of algorithms for analysis. It includes experimental results from research conducted at General Motors' AC Electronics-Defense Research Laboratories and provides a tutorial section to help readers understand the connection between diffraction patterns and original images.

The article examines how adults and children differ in their eye fixation patterns while recognizing and inspecting images with varying levels of clarity. It highlights that children tend to have shorter fixation durations and less effective visual strategies compared to adults, who demonstrate greater skill in selecting informative areas within out-of-focus pictures.

The article presents a real-time visible surface algorithm designed for a display processor capable of rendering complex solid objects at thirty frames per second, highlighting its applications in environmental simulation and architectural modeling. It includes a FORTRAN 5 program that simulates the hardware's performance, with statistical data on its effectiveness detailed in the dissertation.

The article presents a comprehensive review of seismic mapping techniques for reflectors, addressing challenges such as varying velocity models and complex reflector geometries, and consolidates these methods into a unified formula utilizing up and downgoing waves, which can be effectively implemented through difference approximations of the relativistic Schrödinger equation.

The article presents a novel algorithm that enhances the Histogram of Oriented Gradients (HoG) method for pedestrian detection in outdoor environments by utilizing gait motion as a distinctive feature. Experimental results demonstrate its effectiveness in accurately identifying individuals and distinguishing between groups and vehicles, with potential applications in visual surveillance systems for identity tracking across multiple camera views.

The article discusses methods for detecting texture edges, spots, and streaks in digitized images through parallel operations, highlighting the construction of composite outputs from various edge sizes to analyze differently textured regions. It also explores the relationship between these image processing techniques and Gestalt psychology's principles of pictorial pattern organization.

The article by Ugo Montanari discusses methods for the optimal detection of curves in images affected by noise, focusing on techniques in computer vision and applied mathematics for effective curve fitting. It explores the challenges and solutions associated with accurately identifying curves in noisy visual data.

The article explores the effectiveness of human and computer identification of faces using subjective feature descriptions, detailing experiments that analyze face-feature data, assess computer identification capabilities, and evaluate human performance. It identifies a set of 22 distinctive features that can reliably aid in unique face identification, laying the groundwork for future research in real-time human-computer interaction and face recognition technologies.

The article introduces a novel method for standard baseline correction of strong-motion accelerograms using high-pass filtering techniques, which offers a more reliable frequency transfer function compared to traditional parabolic correction methods, regardless of record length.

The article presents a theoretical model of the human eye that incorporates aspheric surfaces for the cornea and crystalline lens, building on Gullstrand's 4-radius model. It discusses the implications of this model on astigmatism and visual performance, utilizing ray tracing to analyze focal lengths and the effects of different optical configurations.

The article discusses the Hough transformation, a computationally efficient method for detecting lines and curves in images, highlighting the advantages of using angle-radius parameters over traditional slope-intercept forms. It also explores the broader applications of this technique in curve fitting and its implications for various fields such as computer vision, image analysis, and pattern recognition.

The article explores how the coherence of real-world scenes impacts the accuracy of object recognition, revealing that even when individuals know what to look for, jumbled scenes hinder perceptual recognition more than coherent ones, suggesting that meaningful context plays a crucial role in scene interpretation.

The article discusses a thesis focused on generating semantic descriptions from drawings of scenes that include shadows, exploring techniques in image analysis, natural language processing, and computer vision to enhance scene understanding and interpretation. It highlights the integration of various fields such as machine learning and data science to improve semantic representation and evaluation.

The article explores spatial-frequency masking in vision by examining how vertical sinusoidal gratings are affected by masking noise with varying frequency bands. It reveals that visual masking occurs primarily with spatial frequencies similar to the grating, indicating the presence of selective channels in the visual system that are tuned to different frequencies.

The article discusses a study on the rehearsal and storage of visual information, examining how different modes of presentation (uniform vs. mixed exposure rates) affect the processing of pictures. The findings suggest that pictures are processed individually based on their presentation duration, with no significant effects from the mode of presentation.

The article explores a second neural mechanism involved in binocular depth discrimination by examining the orientation-selectivity of binocular neurons in the cat's visual cortex, revealing significant interocular differences in receptive field properties that cannot be attributed to measurement errors. This research enhances our understanding of how the brain processes three-dimensional object orientation and depth perception.

The article presents a parallel processing algorithm designed for shrinking binary picture patterns to isolate individual elements, utilizing a minimal two-by-two window approach. It discusses the algorithm's potential applications in image analysis and pattern recognition, as well as its implementation in hardware using large-scale integrated technology, while addressing challenges such as merging disconnections and optimizing processing steps.

The article discusses the significance of textural features in image classification, detailing easily computable features based on gray-tone spatial dependencies and their application in categorizing various types of imagery, including photomicrographs and satellite images. It presents experimental results demonstrating high identification accuracy across different datasets, suggesting the broad applicability of these textural features in image classification tasks.

The article offers a comprehensive overview of statistical methods for pattern recognition and scene analysis, covering topics such as Bayesian decision theory, supervised and unsupervised learning, and various techniques for image classification and analysis within the fields of computer science and machine learning.

This article provides an overview of computerized transverse axial scanning (CT), a highly sensitive imaging technique that utilizes X-ray transmission data taken from multiple angles to create detailed cross-sectional images of the head, allowing for the visualization of soft tissues with similar densities.

The article addresses the challenge of identifying and matching visual objects in photographs based on descriptive specifications, exploring various methodologies in pattern recognition, image analysis, and representation theory to enhance object detection and recognition in computer vision.

The article introduces an innovative approach to visualizing multivariate data by representing points in k-dimensional space (up to 18 dimensions) as cartoon faces, where facial features correspond to the data components. This method enhances the ability to identify patterns and irregularities in the data, while also discussing other graphical representation techniques.

The article discusses the development of computer-oriented mesh generators that utilize curvilinear coordinate systems and bivariate blending-function interpolation techniques to streamline the process of mesh generation for finite element analysis, particularly in complex geometric applications such as automotive design. It highlights methods for constructing invertible maps from unit squares to simply connected planar domains and extends these concepts to three-dimensional solid modeling.

The article presents a straightforward method for identifying significant curvature maxima and minima on digital curves, utilizing concepts from image analysis and differential geometry to enhance angle detection in computer vision applications.

The article introduces an innovative approach to visualizing multivariate data by representing points in k-dimensional space (up to 18 dimensions) as cartoon faces, where facial features correspond to the data components. This method enhances the human ability to recognize patterns and irregularities in complex datasets, while also briefly discussing other graphical representation techniques.

The article explores the challenges of recognizing coarsely sampled and quantized portraits, highlighting that blurring these images through low-pass filtering enhances recognition by effectively removing high-frequency noise. It presents experimental findings that suggest adjacent spectral bands in the image's spectrum are more effective in improving visual recognition than previously understood.

The article discusses the discrete cosine transform (DCT), detailing its definition, computation algorithm, and applications in digital processing, particularly in pattern recognition and Wiener filtering. It also compares the performance of DCT with other orthogonal transforms, including the optimal Karhunen-Loève transform, in terms of rate-distortion criteria across various fields such as image analysis, computer vision, and machine learning.

The article "Computer Processing of Line-Drawing Images" by Herbert Freeman explores the techniques and methodologies involved in the computer processing and analysis of line-drawing images, focusing on areas such as pattern recognition, image manipulation, and computational geometry. It provides insights into the applications of these techniques in fields like computer-aided design and machine vision.

The article explores the relationship between visual fidelity and image encoding by utilizing Shannon's rate-distortion function to evaluate the performance of encoding-transmission systems for monochrome still images. It investigates various measures of distortion and their correspondence to subjective evaluations, ultimately aiming to identify optimal encoding strategies that yield visually satisfactory results.

The article discusses a subdivision algorithm designed for rendering computer-generated images of curved surfaces using three-dimensional patches, which are subdivided into smaller segments for accurate shading and silhouette representation. It highlights the efficiency of this method, particularly for bicubic patches, and its capability to apply textures through photographic mapping.

The article explores the emerging method of lifestyle segmentation in market analysis, detailing its theoretical foundations and practical applications in marketing and advertising. It integrates concepts from various fields, including aesthetics and cognitive science, to enhance understanding of consumer behavior and improve targeted marketing strategies.

The article explores the relationship between the depth of processing of facial images and subsequent recognition memory, demonstrating that deeper processing related to traits like honesty leads to better memory recall compared to superficial processing based on sex. It discusses various studies that support the idea that the nature of processing influences memory retention, regardless of whether the learning was intentional or incidental.

This article discusses the development of a three-dimensional geometric modeling system aimed at enhancing computer vision applications, focusing on image analysis, origin verification, and spatial problem-solving. It highlights the implementation of design concepts in two programs, GEOMED and CRE, which demonstrate camera motion in relation to a static environment.

The article explores the standard methodologies used in computer science for interpreting line drawings as three-dimensional scenes, focusing on techniques in image analysis, pattern recognition, and scene understanding within the context of computer vision and geometric modeling.

The article explores how viewers can quickly and accurately recognize target images in a visual search task when they are provided with the semantic meaning of the images, such as their names, rather than having seen the images themselves. It highlights the interplay between visual perception and cognitive processes in recognizing and interpreting visual information.

The article discusses an approach to computer vision that utilizes polyhedron representation within the framework of inverse graphics, where real images captured by cameras are analyzed to compute detailed geometric models, contrasting with traditional graphics that generate synthetic images from detailed world representations.

The article reviews and analyzes 42 studies on the impact of color coding in visual displays, highlighting its effectiveness in certain conditions while noting potential drawbacks in others. It offers insights for design decisions and identifies gaps in current knowledge related to color visual search and identification performance.

The article discusses a series of lectures focused on the process of vision in both human and electronic systems, drawing from various publications in the fields of human-computer interaction, cognitive science, and visual processing. It aims to provide a convenient juxtaposition of research on visual perception and recognition in both biological and machine contexts.

The article discusses Prof. Dr. Eduard Imhof's insights on the aesthetic and scientific principles of map design, specifically focusing on the optimal positioning of names on maps to enhance legibility and clarity. Drawing from his extensive experience, Imhof emphasizes the importance of determining the best location for lettering in relation to individual features on the map.

The article presents a self-calibration method for non-metric cameras in close-range photogrammetry, addressing various lens distortions and providing interior orientation parameters for individual photographs, while maintaining a minimum control requirement of two horizontal and three vertical points. It discusses the mathematical formulation and implications for fields such as computer vision, digital photogrammetry, and optical metrology.

The article presents a method for automatically collecting powder data from photographs using a microphotometer to measure density at specific angles, followed by a computer program that analyzes diffraction peaks and refines peak positions and intensities. The results demonstrate that this automated approach yields data comparable to traditional methods, showcasing advancements in image analysis and computational imaging techniques.

The article presents a comparative study of three standard approaches for automatic texture classification in terrain analysis, focusing on features derived from the Fourier power spectrum, second-order gray level statistics, and first-order statistics differences. The findings indicate that while one approach generally performed poorly, the other methods yielded comparable results in classifying terrain samples.

This article explores the computation of stereo disparity by establishing correspondences between two images, presenting a cooperative algorithm that effectively extracts information from random-dot stereograms, and discussing its implications for psychophysics and the visual system.

The article explores the concept of "face," distinguishing it from related constructs such as authority and dignity, and examines the dynamics of gaining and losing face within social interactions. It emphasizes the universal significance of face in understanding individual behavior and social functioning, while also touching on its implications across various fields, including aesthetics and visual sciences.

The article discusses a novel approach to picture segmentation that integrates merging and recursive splitting techniques using a tree traversal algorithm, resulting in enhanced processing speed while optimizing memory usage. It provides detailed descriptions of the data structure and includes implementation examples, contributing to advancements in image analysis and computer vision.

This article explores a heuristic search method for detecting edges and contours in noisy images, focusing on minimizing a merit function that represents edge properties. It discusses the application of well-known search algorithms and dynamic programming to improve computational efficiency, while also presenting experimental results that demonstrate the effectiveness of the approach in extracting shape contours and separating touching objects.

The article provides an introduction to adaptive array antenna systems, highlighting their significance in radar communications for automatically steering nulls to mitigate interference and enhance signal detection. It discusses the mechanics of a real-time adaptive receiver-processor, focusing on the Howells-Applebaum analog servo-control-loop processor and its mathematical foundations for beam-forming and null steering.

The article discusses the process of detecting straight picture edges in digitized scenes with polyhedra, emphasizing the importance of collinearity of feature points and the role of grey levels in image analysis. It highlights the computational challenges and methodologies involved in edge detection within the context of scene analysis programs.

The article investigates the timing of perceptual asymmetries in face recognition, examining whether these occur during early or late stages of processing. Through a series of experiments, it reveals that while both hemispheres can access short-lived visual traces early on, specialized functions in one hemisphere are necessary for maintaining and comparing more permanent memory representations of faces.

The article discusses a novel stereoscopic method for creating 3-D displays using computer-generated holograms, detailing the process of synthesizing composite holograms through holographic stereograms. It highlights the efficiency of this approach in terms of computation time and wide viewing angles, while also addressing the characteristics of the reconstructed images and presenting an experimental example.

The article discusses a program designed to recognize real roads, intersections, and road-like objects in satellite images, with applications extending beyond roads to include various objects such as rivers and veins. It highlights the program's capabilities in remote sensing and image analysis, emphasizing its relevance in fields like computer vision and geodesy.

The article discusses the use of Fourier descriptors (FDs) for shape discrimination in image processing and pattern recognition, highlighting their properties and effectiveness in measuring differences between boundary curves. It reviews two types of FDs, explores their application in obtaining object skeletons, and presents experimental results related to character recognition and machine parts analysis.

The article discusses advanced models of light reflection used in computer-generated imagery, focusing on the accurate simulation of highlights on three-dimensional objects based on experimental measurements of real surface reflections. It highlights improvements over traditional models by incorporating variations in light source direction and surface characteristics, particularly in distinguishing between metallic and non-metallic surfaces, with examples illustrated through still frames from animated sequences.

The article explores the developmental differences in facial recognition abilities between young children and adults, highlighting that children under 10 struggle with recognizing faces presented upside down and are easily deceived by disguises. It suggests that the maturation of the right cerebral hemisphere plays a crucial role in the ability to encode orientation-specific configurational aspects of faces.

The article discusses research on a system that enables vehicles to navigate cluttered environments autonomously using an onboard camera for visual guidance, focusing on rapid and reliable techniques to detect and locate obstacles without identifying them.

The article discusses two innovative techniques for image matching: parametric correspondence, which aligns images with three-dimensional symbolic reference maps using an analytic camera model, and chamfer matching, which efficiently compares shape fragments based on linear dimensions. These methods enhance the accuracy of image matching by reducing ambiguity associated with traditional correlation techniques.

The article discusses the CIE 1976 color-difference formulae recommended by the International Commission on Illumination, highlighting their uniformity in color spaces and the factors influencing the choice of formula in various applications, including industry practices and familiarity. It also explores the relationship between these formulae and visual judgments, emphasizing their relevance across multiple scientific fields.

The article introduces a novel phase unwrapping algorithm that integrates derivative and principal value information into an adaptive numerical integration method, demonstrating high reliability and compatibility with standard homomorphic signal processors, with applications across various fields such as image analysis, computer vision, and computational imaging.

The article discusses a new algorithm for fast boundary detection in digitized images, utilizing a set of orthogonal functions to enhance edge detection criteria. The proposed method demonstrates significant improvements in efficiency and accuracy compared to existing techniques, with only a slight increase in computational cost.

The article presents a novel algorithm for addressing the stereoscopic matching problem in human stereo vision, outlining a five-step process that includes image filtering, zero-crossing localization, and disparity range analysis to achieve correspondence. It also establishes a theoretical framework that aligns with existing psychophysical and neurophysiological data on stereopsis, while making predictions for future experimental validation.

The article presents findings from four experiments that demonstrate how the time required to scan visual images increases with distance, suggesting that visual imagery preserves metric spatial information. It discusses the implications of these results for understanding cognitive processes related to image scanning and the potential confounding factors in previous studies.

The article provides a comprehensive overview of the current state and recent developments in segmentation research, addressing key aspects such as problem definition, research design, data collection, and analysis methods, while also highlighting future research directions across various fields including image, market, and user segmentation.

The article discusses the role of early visual processing in recovering intrinsic scene characteristics—such as range, orientation, reflectance, and incident illumination—from images, emphasizing the importance of these characteristics for higher-level analysis and human perception. It explores the challenges of extracting this information from light-intensity images and proposes methods to leverage physical imaging constraints for effective scene understanding.

The article explores visual object recognition in patients with right- or left-hemisphere lesions, utilizing two tasks that focus on perceptual and semantic categorization. It discusses the observed impairments in the right hemisphere and proposes a tentative model for the cerebral organization of categorical stages in object recognition.

The article discusses a method for computing the discrete cosine transform (DCT) using an N-point discrete Fourier transform (DFT) algorithm, which offers a computational speed advantage by being approximately twice as fast as traditional methods that utilize a 2N DFT. It highlights applications in various fields such as image analysis, computer vision, and digital signal processing.

The article discusses the development of a model-based vision system designed to accurately identify the position and orientation of complex curved industrial parts in noisy gray-level images, utilizing a compact representation of image data for effective model formation and matching under varying conditions.

The article explores how individuals can selectively attend to specific targets within visual arrays without eye movements, highlighting the role of trial-to-trial attention switching and the utility of attention operating characteristics in analyzing simultaneous compatibility tasks. It bridges concepts from cognitive science, psychology, and computer vision to enhance understanding of visual perception and processing.

The article explores the separation of visual axes in apposition compound eyes of various insects and crustaceans, focusing on the relationship between interommatidial angles, facet diameters, and the effective intensity of visual function. It discusses how these factors influence spatial resolution and sensitivity, while presenting maps of different arthropod habitats to illustrate regional variations in ocular structure and function.

This article provides an overview of a textbook designed for introductory courses in remote sensing, covering essential topics such as photographic systems, airphoto interpretation, photogrammetry, and various imaging techniques including thermography and multispectral scanning. It emphasizes the integration of remote sensing with fields like computer vision, spatial analysis, and planetary sciences.

The article discusses Lanczos filtering, a Fourier-based method for filtering digital data that effectively reduces amplitude Gibbs oscillation using "sigma factors." It highlights the simplicity of the technique, which requires only two inputs to assess filter response quality, and provides comparisons with other filters through one-dimensional examples.

This article presents an unsupervised image segmentation technique that utilizes a mathematical-pattern recognition model to cluster imagery without the need for training prototypes. The method focuses on retaining the most relevant features while eliminating noise, ultimately maximizing the fidelity of the segmented images through iterative clustering and evaluation of parameter quality.

The article discusses the use of quad trees for image representation, detailing algorithms for efficiently processing images by building boundary polygons and coloring their interiors. It highlights the computational efficiency of these algorithms in relation to the number of nodes and polygon characteristics, making them suitable for applications in image analysis and digital image processing.

The article discusses the analysis of first-order difference pictures (FODP) derived from image sequences of real-world scenes, focusing on identifying and separating moving objects from stationary components. It presents a method for recognizing image objects without specific prior knowledge, utilizing accumulative difference pictures to enhance scene interpretation and motion analysis.

The article explores recognition memory for faces, revealing that individuals have poorer memory for faces that are typical or prototype-like compared to those with unusual features, across various learning conditions and time intervals. It also discusses the implications of these findings for current cognitive models of memory and classification.

The article introduces a novel method for texture analysis that utilizes generalized co-occurrence matrices (GCM) to capture the spatial distribution of local features in unsegmented textures, highlighting its applications in natural image analysis and computer vision. It discusses the formulation of GCMs based on specific constraints and presents experimental findings that demonstrate their effectiveness in image representation and pattern recognition.

The article presents a novel technique called photometric stereo, which determines surface orientation by varying the direction of incident illumination while keeping the viewing angle constant, allowing for accurate surface analysis using intensity values from a single image element across multiple views. This method overcomes challenges associated with traditional stereo imaging by eliminating the need for correspondence between different views.

The article presents a theory of edge detection that analyzes intensity changes in images across various scales using second derivative Gaussian filters, leading to the identification of zero-crossing segments as oriented primitives. It further discusses how these segments, derived from surface discontinuities, can be combined to create a raw primal sketch, providing insights into image representation and psychophysical findings in the context of computer vision and image analysis.

The article introduces a novel photometric stereo technique that determines surface orientation from multiple images by varying the direction of incident illumination while keeping the viewing angle constant. This method leverages radiance values from a single location to provide sufficient information for surface orientation analysis, with applications illustrated through synthesized examples.

The article evaluates the effectiveness of four texture analysis algorithms—spatial gray level dependence method (SGLDM), run length (GLRLM), difference (GLDM), and power spectral method (PSM)—in automatic texture discrimination, focusing on their ability to capture texture context information. It compares the algorithms using Markov-generated textures and extends the analysis to a broader class of translation stationary random fields, concluding that SGLDM is the most powerful among the methods assessed.

The article discusses the challenges of image texture analysis and evaluates various existing methods, highlighting a new approach that utilizes simple energy transforms for improved segmentation accuracy. It emphasizes the effectiveness of small mask filtering techniques and their alignment with human visual processing, achieving notable classification results across different texture types.

The article presents an algorithm for matching images of real-world scenes by analyzing geometrical disparity, which aids in the partial reconstruction of three-dimensional structures. It details a method for selecting candidate points in images, estimating matches based on similarity, and refining these estimates using relaxation labeling techniques to enhance accuracy in disparity analysis.

The article presents a diagrammatic and self-consistent approach to the Anderson localization problem in dimensions less than or equal to two, focusing on the density response function of non-interacting particles in a random potential. It discusses the implications of time-reversal invariance on the breakdown of perturbation theory and provides insights into the behavior of static polarizability and dynamical conductivity in weakly disordered systems.

The article presents a novel method for rapid texture identification in images, utilizing a "texture energy" approach that involves minimal convolutions with small integer coefficient masks and a moving-window absolute average operation. This technique enhances pixel classification and scene segmentation while ensuring rotation invariance and efficient processing on general-purpose digital computers.

This article reviews various techniques for digital picture encoding, discussing statistical models and psychophysical elements relevant to coding, and providing detailed examples of three typical systems. It also forecasts a promising future for new encoding systems based on emerging technologies and integrated circuits.

The article provides a comprehensive overview of algorithms used in graphics and image processing, covering topics such as digitization, transformations, filtering, segmentation, and three-dimensional graphics. It explores various forms of pictorial data, input and display devices, and includes discussions on data structures, curve fitting, and surface representation, making it a valuable resource for understanding the intersection of computer science and visual data analysis.

This article provides a comprehensive review of various image data compression techniques, ranging from basic pulse code modulation to advanced predictive and hybrid methods, highlighting their applications and the impact of channel errors. It emphasizes the fundamentals of these algorithms while offering a bibliography for readers seeking further theoretical and experimental insights.

The article provides a comprehensive overview of computer image processing and recognition, covering key topics such as image formation, enhancement, segmentation, and reconstruction, as well as advanced techniques in machine learning and computer vision for effective image analysis and recognition.

The article discusses a novel algorithm for depth estimation in three-dimensional image processing, focusing on an edge-based correlation method that enhances the accuracy and reliability of depth data for various applications such as navigation and industrial automation. The approach involves extracting edge descriptions from paired images, establishing connectivity, and refining correspondences to generate a comprehensive disparity map of the scene.

The article "From Images to Surfaces" explores a computational approach to transforming two-dimensional images into three-dimensional representations by analyzing visual information processing systems, both biological and artificial. It outlines a framework that includes distinct levels of theory and implementation, detailing a methodology for surface interpolation that progresses from initial image analysis to the creation of explicit 3-D models.

The article presents a mathematical model of visual flow that explains how observers perceive and interact with moving objects, detailing their sensitivity to various aspects such as approach angle, velocity changes, and collision timing. Through a series of experiments, it highlights the complexities of human visual perception in relation to motion detection and object tracking.

The article introduces the background field method in gauge theories, reviewing conventional functional techniques and discussing Feynman rules renormalization, with a specific example of computing the Yang-Mills β function.

The article discusses David Marr's influential work "Vision," which presents a comprehensive framework for understanding visual perception through computational investigation, highlighting the importance of different levels of analysis in studying how humans process visual information. It emphasizes the ongoing relevance of Marr's ideas in neuroscience and cognitive science, inspiring new generations of researchers in the field.

The article presents a novel fast-Fourier-transform method for analyzing fringe patterns in topography and interferometry, enabling automatic discrimination of elevation and depression in objects without the need for moving components, thus offering improved accuracy and sensitivity compared to traditional fringe-contour techniques.

The article introduces EULDPH, a novel technique for estimating depths from magnetic data without relying on predefined geological models, allowing for broader application across various scenarios. It emphasizes the importance of the interpreter's experience and understanding of equivalent strata for successful implementation, while detailing the theoretical foundation, computational algorithm, and practical applications of the method.

The article "Computational Stereo" by Stephen T. Barnard and Martin A. Fischler provides an in-depth exploration of stereo imaging techniques within the fields of computer vision and computational imaging, focusing on scene modeling, multi-view geometry, and the mathematical principles underlying stereoscopic processing. It serves as a comprehensive survey of the advancements and methodologies in computational stereo, highlighting its applications in image analysis and digital image processing.

The article analyzes phase unwrapping algorithms used in high-resolution imaging through turbulent atmospheres, highlighting their role in calculating and averaging phase angles from image Fourier transforms to mitigate atmospheric distortions. It discusses the necessity of eliminating ambiguities in phase data before averaging, a process known as tracking or unwrapping, and references related work in homomorphic signal processing.

The article discusses the use of parameterized models in facial animation, highlighting their ability to generate realistic and easily manipulable images of human faces using a minimal number of parameters. It explores applications in computer science, human-computer interaction, and machine learning, particularly in areas like facial recognition and avatar animation.

The article discusses the significance of detecting multicollinearity in multiple regression analysis, highlighting its potential negative impact on estimated coefficients. It emphasizes the importance of training researchers in identifying multicollinearity through methods such as examining latent roots, vectors, and variance inflation factors (VIFs).

The article investigates how changes in facial presentation, such as angle and expression, affect recognition accuracy and response times in face recognition tasks. Through two experiments, it reveals that unchanged faces are recognized more quickly and accurately than those with alterations, highlighting the interplay between visual and non-visual coding processes in face recognition.

This article provides a comprehensive survey of automated visual inspection in industrial settings, categorizing various publications and reports by their focus areas, including design considerations, commercially available systems, and specific applications such as printed circuit boards and integrated chips. It also includes a selectively annotated bibliography of tools related to image analysis, pattern recognition, and machine vision technologies.

The article introduces the Local-Feature-Focus method, a novel approach for locating partially visible two-dimensional objects, particularly complex industrial parts, by utilizing clusters of mutually consistent features and templates for verification. This technique enhances efficiency by focusing on key features derived from detailed analyses of computer-aided design (CAD) models, while also incorporating algorithms for rotational and mirror symmetry to improve object localization.

The article presents a novel image encoding technique using the Laplacian Pyramid, which employs local operators of varying scales to achieve data compression by removing pixel-to-pixel correlations and quantizing the difference image. This iterative process enhances salient features and is well-suited for analysis tasks, supported by fast algorithms for coding and decoding.

The article explores advanced techniques for edge detection in images, focusing on variational methods to optimize the identification of intensity changes while minimizing false positives. It discusses the formulation of edge detection operators that capture desirable properties and the integration of various outputs to enhance image analysis and pattern recognition in computer vision.

The article provides a comprehensive survey of various range finding techniques used in computer vision and robotics, highlighting their applicability and limitations in acquiring range data for scene analysis and three-dimensional object configuration. It emphasizes the importance of these techniques in non-contact determination and remote sensing applications.

The article compares various interpolation methods for image resampling, evaluating their effectiveness in preserving image quality during transformations such as rotation. It specifically analyzes five techniques—nearest neighbor, linear, cubic B-spline, and two high-resolution splines—with a focus on their frequency response and the impact of sampling point alignment on the final image quality.

The article examines the statistical distributions of two-dimensional discrete cosine transform (DCT) coefficients in image coding, revealing that many images are best represented by a Gaussian distribution for DC coefficients and a Laplacian distribution for others. It also demonstrates through simulations that these assumptions lead to improved signal-to-noise ratios and better alignment with theoretical predictions.

The article discusses a method for creating and refining volumetric representations of objects using occluding contours from multiple viewpoints, focusing on a specific representation called "volume segment" that balances the need for detailed surface description with the ability to modify the volume. It highlights the significance of this approach in image analysis, 3D vision, and computer vision.

The article discusses ACRONYM, a model-based system designed for vision and manipulation tasks, which utilizes three-dimensional geometric models to derive predictions and interpretations from two-dimensional images. It highlights the system's ability to generate instructions for measurement and analysis, showcasing preliminary examples of its interpretations of aerial images.

The article provides an overview of "The Foucault Reader," highlighting key concepts in Foucault's philosophy and including previously unpublished material, while also touching on various interdisciplinary applications such as natural language processing, digital scholarship, and sociology.

The article discusses the use of fractal functions to effectively represent and compute natural shapes in images, such as mountains and trees, by modeling their surfaces. It highlights the advantages of fractals in generating realistic textures and their applications in image analysis, scene understanding, and pattern recognition.

The article investigates how abrupt visual onsets influence selective attention during visual search tasks, demonstrating that items with sudden appearances capture attention more effectively than those that are gradually revealed. Through a series of experiments, the study confirms that this attentional advantage is not merely due to sensory or perceptual factors, but rather highlights the significance of temporal discontinuity in visual processing.

The article discusses a method for obtaining high-resolution surface profiles of 3-D diffuse objects using automated phase-measuring profilometry, which employs a shearing polarization interferometer and phase-shifting techniques to achieve surface height resolutions better than 10 μm. This approach is applicable in various fields, including industrial quality control, robotics, and computer graphics, and demonstrates effectiveness even with low-density sensing arrays.

The article discusses a method for analyzing and classifying textures based on their properties at varying resolutions, highlighting how finer details diminish at coarser resolutions. It explores the use of fractal analysis to quantify these changes and facilitate texture comparison and classification, while also addressing the directional properties of textures.

The article provides an overview of extended Gaussian images, highlighting their utility in representing surface shapes and their computation from needle maps or depth data. It discusses their applications in machine vision tasks such as object recognition and attitude determination, while also addressing the complexities of nonconvex objects with illustrative examples.

The article provides a comprehensive overview of laser remote sensing, covering its fundamental principles, including electromagnetic theory and quantum physics, as well as its applications in atmospheric research, hydrography, and digital imaging. It discusses the operation of laser-based sensors, the analysis of lidar return signals, and the integration of these technologies in fields such as geography, computer vision, and earth science.

The article explores a method for recognizing and localizing objects using sparse range or tactile data from sensors, modeling these objects as polyhedra with up to six degrees of freedom. It presents an efficient algorithm that discards inconsistent hypotheses about object surface pairings based on geometric constraints, demonstrating its effectiveness through simulations and performance data.

The article presents an algorithm that enhances ray tracing efficiency by utilizing an octree-based space subdivision method, which minimizes the number of object-ray intersection calculations required. This approach enables faster access to nodes and improves the detection of intersected rays, making it feasible to trace complex scenes on medium- to small-scale computers.

The article presents a real-time algorithm designed for the accurate detection of QRS complexes in ECG signals, achieving a 99.3% detection rate by utilizing digital analysis techniques and adaptive threshold adjustments to minimize false positives from interference. The algorithm's effectiveness is enhanced by a specialized bandpass filter, allowing for increased sensitivity in various heart rate morphologies.

The article presents a model for human visual-motion sensing that incorporates psychophysical constraints, detailing a two-stage process involving spatial-frequency-tuned, direction-selective sensors that encode motion direction and resolve motion components across various frequencies. This model is supported by examples such as apparent motion and coherent gratings, aligning with qualitative perceptions of motion.

This article defines the three-dimensional object recognition problem within computer vision, explores fundamental concepts related to it, and reviews existing literature, with a focus on the use of range images for data acquisition and processing.

The article introduces a stereo matching algorithm that employs dynamic programming to efficiently find correspondences between rectified left and right images through intra- and inter-scanline searches. By utilizing edge-delimited intervals and a novel interval-based similarity metric, the method enhances consistency across 2D planes and has been tested on various image types, including urban aerial scenes.

This article reviews recent advancements in digital video signal coding over the past four years, focusing on displacement estimation algorithms, predictive transform developments for broadcast and video conferencing, and motion adaptive frame interpolation, while also addressing unresolved challenges in the field.

The article discusses advancements in second-generation image-coding techniques aimed at reducing the bit representation of images while maintaining high fidelity. It highlights the evolution of compression ratios, recent insights from neuroscience on visual perception, and introduces two main classes of methods that utilize local operators for improved contour and texture descriptions, with the potential to achieve compression ratios of up to 100:1.

The article presents a linear recognition algorithm for cographs, which are undirected graphs characterized by the absence of induced paths on four vertices. It discusses the unique tree representation of cographs, known as cotrees, and highlights the algorithm's efficiency in solving various computational problems related to graph theory.

The article discusses phase-shifting speckle interferometry, a technique that utilizes high-frequency phase data from speckle patterns to quantitatively measure deformations, such as those caused by tilting in metal plates. By employing computer-controlled data processing, this method enhances accuracy in various applications, including biomedical imaging and optical metrology, while effectively reducing noise from random fluctuations.

The article discusses advancements in the Marr-Poggio computational model of the human stereo system, highlighting modifications and refinements based on psychophysical experiments and applications to natural images, particularly aerial photographs. It presents a new version of the model that incorporates these updates and demonstrates its performance through a series of computational experiments.

The article discusses the PMF algorithm, a novel approach to solving the stereo correspondence problem by imposing a limit on disparity gradients, which enhances the balance between disambiguation and surface variability. It highlights the algorithm's effectiveness through various natural and artificial stereograms, while also addressing its theoretical foundations and comparing it to existing methods in the literature.

The article explores the distinction between the rapid, preattentive processing of spatial information ("where") and the slower, attention-dependent recognition of specific features ("what") in visual perception, highlighting the complexities of scene interpretation and the cognitive processes involved in visual recognition.

The article discusses a statistical approach to reconstructing images from noisy pixel data using a Markov random field model, emphasizing the importance of local pixel similarities. It proposes a simplified iterative method to address the computational challenges of image reconstruction and explores potential applications in fields such as image analysis and computer vision.

This article presents a theoretical model for understanding face recognition, outlining seven distinct types of information derived from faces and exploring the cognitive processes involved in recognizing familiar faces. It integrates findings from various studies, including laboratory experiments and observations of individuals with cerebral injuries, to clarify the similarities and differences between face recognition and other forms of recognition, such as object and word recognition.

"Robot Vision" is a comprehensive guide to the field of computer vision, emphasizing the image formation process and its applications in robotics. The book integrates theoretical concepts with practical examples, covering recent research and providing a framework for future advancements in machine processing and pattern recognition.

The article explores Thomas Nagel's examination of the dual perspectives of human experience: the detached, objective viewpoint and the subjective, personal standpoint. It discusses how these seemingly irreconcilable views can coexist and influence various philosophical issues, including ethics, identity, and the nature of reality, while critiquing the reductionist tendencies in contemporary analytic philosophy.

This article explores the application of subband coding techniques, traditionally used for speech encoding, to image compression. It details the use of two-dimensional quadrature mirror filters for optimal bit allocation among subbands, adaptive techniques for variance-based bit distribution, and compares the signal-to-noise ratios of the resulting coded images with those produced by discrete cosine transform and vector quantization methods.

The article explores the process of edge detection in image analysis, focusing on the characterization of intensity changes and the necessity of numerical differentiation. It discusses the theoretical aspects of edge detection, including the properties of minimal uncertainty filters, relationships among 2-D differential operators, and the geometrical topological study of zero crossings in the context of computer vision and image segmentation.

The article discusses the challenges and methodologies involved in recognizing and locating rigid 3-D objects in space, emphasizing the importance of effective representation and processing paradigms for applications in robotics navigation. It explores the use of various algorithms and data types, including laser and ultrasound, to construct object representations and implement efficient location strategies based on shape and rigidity constraints.

The article explores the challenges of estimating displacement vector fields from image sequences, emphasizing the need for smoothness constraints to improve accuracy, particularly at occluding contours. It discusses the concept of "oriented smoothness" introduced by Nagel, which limits variations in specific directions, and presents findings on how these constraints can be effectively formulated and evaluated.

This article provides a comprehensive survey of texture mapping, a key technique in high-quality image synthesis that enhances the visual richness of raster-scan images with minimal computational cost. It covers the fundamentals of texture mapping, including geometric warping onto surfaces and filtering to prevent aliasing, and includes an extensive bibliography for further reading.

The article presents an algorithm for determining the least-squares solution of the translation vector T in the relationship between two 3-D point sets, utilizing singular value decomposition (SVD) for improved computational efficiency compared to previous methods. It highlights applications in various fields such as computer vision, geometric modeling, and machine learning.

This article explores the significance of mental imagery in consumer behavior, highlighting its role as a multisensory processing mode in working memory and its effects on cognitive elaboration. It also proposes research directions for understanding the relationship between imagery and consumption behaviors, while reviewing various methods for studying imagery in the context of information processing.

The article provides a comprehensive introduction to the computer processing of remotely-sensed images, covering fundamental principles of remote sensing, various sensing platforms and sensors, digital image processing techniques, and advanced topics such as classification and filtering methods. It serves as a detailed guide for understanding the interaction of electromagnetic radiation with Earth's materials and the subsequent processing of the acquired data for applications in fields like environmental monitoring and resource management.

The article introduces a novel method for modeling and segmenting noisy textured images using Gibbs Random Fields, focusing on hierarchical Gibbs distributions and dynamic programming algorithms based on the maximum a posteriori criterion. It also addresses computational challenges by proposing sub-optimal approximations and presents a parameter estimation technique, supported by examples demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed approach in image analysis and processing.

The article presents a straightforward algorithm for calculating phase differences in digital phase-shifting interferometry, which compensates for errors by measuring intensity distributions across a pupil with at least three different phase shifts. It highlights applications in various fields such as computer science, mechanical engineering, and precision measurement.

The article presents a fast and efficient voxel traversal algorithm designed for ray tracing in 3D space, which minimizes computational expense by reducing the number of ray-object intersection tests required. By utilizing a simple approach that involves only two floating point comparisons to navigate between neighboring voxels, the algorithm significantly enhances rendering performance while maintaining high fidelity in image generation.

The article presents a model for extracting optical flow using spatiotemporal motion-energy filters, aligning with current understanding of motion perception in neurophysiology and psychophysics. It effectively addresses the aperture problem and simulates psychophysical data, demonstrating its relevance to image analysis and computer vision.

The article discusses a novel approach to solving the correspondence problem in motion analysis by tracking feature points across monocular image sequences. It presents an iterative optimization algorithm that accounts for object inertia and smooth motion, effectively handling nonrigid objects and occlusions, and demonstrates its effectiveness through various synthetic and real-world scenarios.

The article discusses a method for recognizing objects in three-dimensional space using range images, detailing a three-stage process that involves segmenting the images into surface patches, classifying these patches based on statistical analysis, and merging compatible patches to form coherent object representations. This approach has been successfully applied to a variety of real and synthetic datasets, highlighting its effectiveness in computer vision and image analysis.

The article provides a comprehensive overview of the fundamentals of digital image processing, covering key topics such as image perception, sampling and quantization, various transforms, enhancement techniques, filtering and restoration methods, and applications in computer vision and data compression. It serves as a foundational resource for understanding the principles and techniques used in digital imaging and analysis.

The article explores the relationships between the colors of the JHKL photometric systems from various observatories, proposing a standardized system based on existing data to derive intrinsic colors for different stellar types. It discusses the transformation of color indices and the adjustment of passbands to align synthetic estimates with observed data, facilitating improved accuracy in photometric measurements across multiple fields, including astronomy and optical metrology.

The article presents a three-layer neural network designed to perform complete discrete 2-D Gabor transforms for image analysis, segmentation, and compression, utilizing a combination of fixed and adjustable weights to achieve efficient spectral representations. This approach enables significant compression ratios and effective segmentation through biologically inspired techniques, enhancing the capabilities of image processing and machine learning applications.

This article explores the science of fractal images, covering their characterization and simulation in nature, algorithms for generating random fractals, and the application of fractals in modeling real-world landscapes and chaotic systems. It also delves into the visualization techniques and computational methods used in fractal analysis and image processing.

The article presents an algorithm for image segmentation that utilizes variable-order surface fitting to partition image arrays into coherent regions, enabling the reconstruction of these areas with bivariate functions. It details a method that combines initial coarse segmentation through surface curvature labeling with an iterative region-growing approach, demonstrating effective performance across various intensity images.

This article provides a comprehensive review of vector quantization techniques for digital image encoding, exploring various types of quantizers such as spatial, predictive, and hybrid, and highlighting their effectiveness when integrated with traditional image coding methods across different applications.

The article reviews the phenomenon of face recognition impairment due to stimulus inversion, highlighting that this effect suggests a specialized processing mechanism for upright faces compared to other visual stimuli. It examines various studies involving memory tasks, matching tasks, and brain asymmetries, concluding that upright faces are processed distinctly, underscoring the unique challenges posed by inverted faces in recognition tasks.

The article discusses the design and implementation of a highly pipelined edge detection filter using the Sobel operator, developed with a 2-micron CMOS technology. It highlights the chip's capability to perform real-time gradient magnitude and direction computations at a speed of approximately 200 million additions per second, demonstrating its effectiveness in image analysis and computer vision applications.

The article reviews the concept of feature geometry in phonological theory, emphasizing the hierarchical organization of distinctive features within segments, particularly the dichotomy between Laryngeal and Place nodes. It discusses how these features relate to articulation and provides evidence from various phonological processes, including assimilation and the Obligatory Contour Principle.

The article explores the cascade processes involved in picture identification, proposing that naming pictures may involve continuous information transmission across different types of knowledge—structural, semantic, and phonological—rather than discrete processing stages. It presents evidence from normal observers and patients with acquired naming disorders to support this model and discusses its implications for understanding visual object recognition disorders.

The article presents a novel iterative blind deconvolution method for recovering two convolved functions, detailing the technique and showcasing results from its computational implementation, along with potential applications in fields such as image analysis, digital signal processing, and computer vision.

The article discusses the application of back-propagation networks for recognizing handwritten digits, highlighting a specialized network architecture that requires minimal data preprocessing. It reports a 1% error rate with a 9% rejection rate on U.S. Postal Service zipcode digits, showcasing advancements in machine learning and computer vision for digit recognition.

The article presents a guided search model as an alternative to Treisman's feature integration model for visual search, demonstrating that subjects can more efficiently locate targets defined by conjunctions of color and form when using a guided approach. It highlights that reaction times for conjunction searches are shallower than expected, suggesting that attention can be directed using simple feature information, leading to improved search efficiency.

The article discusses the mechanisms of visual pattern analyzers in the human visual system, detailing how sensory data is processed to extract meaningful information about the environment. It reviews psychophysical experiments, mathematical models, and neurophysiological evidence related to various aspects of vision, including orientation and motion, while also providing foundational material on signal theory and Fourier analysis.

The article provides a comprehensive review of recent advancements in stereo correspondence techniques for 3D scene reconstruction, categorizing algorithms based on imaging geometry, matching primitives, and computational methods, while also evaluating their performance on various test images and suggesting future research directions.

The article discusses the realization of general nondiffracting beams using computer-generated holograms, demonstrating how these fields can be approximated with lasers and specific holographic techniques. It highlights experimental results in forming arbitrary-order Bessel beams and nonsymmetric beams, while also addressing the coding and fabrication methods necessary for achieving desired diffraction efficiencies.

The article discusses the box plot as an effective visual tool for exploratory data analysis, highlighting its ability to summarize and compare data groups through key statistical measures such as median and quartiles. It emphasizes the box plot's role in enhancing the interpretation of complex data, serving as a valuable complement to traditional tables in data visualization.

The article discusses a method for achieving rotation-, scale-, and translation-invariant image recognition using Zernike moments, which are orthogonal complex moments that facilitate effective feature selection and reconstruction. It highlights the advantages of these moments in image classification and demonstrates their superiority through experimental validation.

The article presents a computational method for multichannel texture analysis using localized spatial filters, specifically two-dimensional Gabor functions, to model and differentiate textures based on their spatial frequency characteristics. By encoding images into multiple frequency and orientation channels, the approach enhances the detection of textural boundaries and variations, facilitating improved image analysis and interpretation.

The article presents a model for human preattentive texture perception, detailing a three-stage process that includes convolution with linear filters, localized inhibition among neural responses, and texture-boundary detection. The model effectively predicts salience boundaries in grayscale images and has been validated through computer implementation against classic psychophysical stimuli, demonstrating strong alignment with experimental measurements of texture discriminability.

The article investigates the hypothesis that abrupt visual onsets capture attention automatically, contrasting voluntary and automatic allocation of attention through a series of experiments. It explores the mechanisms behind this phenomenon, suggesting that while attention can be efficiently allocated in response to visual onsets, it does not always align with traditional criteria for automaticity.

The article discusses the challenges posed by speckle in radar images for class discrimination and evaluates various adaptive speckle filters designed to enhance image quality by preserving texture and edge information. It highlights the effectiveness of these filters in managing scene heterogeneity and introduces practical criteria to improve their performance in synthetic aperture radar (SAR) imaging.

This article provides a comprehensive survey of the advancements in online handwriting recognition, highlighting key developments in technology, algorithms, and commercial applications, while contrasting online and offline recognition methods. It reviews a wide range of literature, including journal articles and patents, to discuss various challenges and techniques in the field.

This article explores the microstructure of the Bruce & Young (1986) functional model of face recognition through an interactive activation implementation, presenting a simulation that accounts for various findings related to semantic and repetition priming. It also offers speculative predictions based on the model and suggests directions for future empirical research in the field of face recognition and computer vision.

The two-volume set "Computer and Robot Vision" offers a comprehensive exploration of computer vision, addressing various aspects such as image processing, feature extraction, and advanced techniques like motion analysis and model-based systems, making it a valuable resource for professionals in computer science and robotics.

This article provides a selective review of nearly two decades of research on semantic priming effects in visual word recognition, highlighting how context influences word recognition and summarizing key empirical findings and theoretical developments in the field of psycholinguistics and cognitive science.

The article discusses a method for assessing significant changes in regional cerebral activity using statistical parametric maps (SPMs) in functional PET imaging, highlighting the importance of accounting for smoothness and multiple comparisons in the analysis. It presents a theoretical framework and empirical applications to improve the reliability of identifying significant changes in brain activity.

The article discusses the construction of a unique weak solution to a nonlinear partial differential equation that describes the evolution of level sets over time according to their mean curvature, enabling the definition of generalized motion by curvature. It also explores various geometric properties and potential pathologies associated with this evolution, with applications in fields such as mechanical engineering, computer science, and applied mathematics.

This book presents a comprehensive overview of contemporary research in computational models of visual processing, featuring over twenty new contributions that explore various aspects of the visual system, including neurophysiology, psychophysics, and the analysis of motion, color, and depth. The introductory chapter by Edward Adelson and James Bergen sets the stage for subsequent sections that delve into topics such as neural function, detection, and 3D shape, all linked by a consistent focus on the relationship between empirical data and modeling.

The article presents a novel approach to visual object recognition that utilizes linear combinations of 2D images to represent 3D objects, demonstrating that this method can effectively capture sharp edges and smooth contours. It also discusses the ability to distinguish rigid transformations and proposes three alternative methods for matching transformations to model images.

The article discusses efficient algorithms for continuous image representation and interpolation using B-splines, detailing both direct and indirect transforms. It emphasizes the implementation of these transforms through linear operators and filtering techniques, highlighting the advantages of the proposed methods in maintaining image resolution during interpolation.

The article provides a comprehensive overview of image registration techniques, which are essential for aligning images taken under varying conditions, such as different times or viewpoints. It categorizes these techniques into three major types based on the sources of misalignment and discusses their applications in fields like medical imaging, satellite monitoring, and real-time scene recognition, while emphasizing the importance of understanding the characteristics of each variation to select the most suitable registration method.

The article discusses various methods for combining multiple classifiers to enhance handwriting recognition, categorizing solutions based on the information available from different classifiers. It presents four distinct approaches, demonstrating significant performance improvements in recognizing unconstrained handwritten numerals, with experimental results indicating high reliability and accuracy.

The article presents a rapid automated algorithm for the three-dimensional alignment and reslicing of PET images, utilizing voxel-by-voxel analysis to minimize variance without the need for external fiducial markers. Validation studies demonstrate its effectiveness in aligning images from various positions with minimal positional errors, making it a valuable tool for retrospective analysis in medical imaging.

The article presents a CFAR adaptive matched filter detector designed for radar target detection using an antenna array, detailing an algorithm that simplifies the generalized likelihood-ratio test (GLRT) while analyzing performance for signals aligned and misaligned with the look direction.

The article "Tree Visualization with Tree-Maps" by Ben Shneiderman presents a two-dimensional space-filling approach for visualizing hierarchical data using tree-maps, highlighting its applications in fields such as data science, information visualization, and forest research. It discusses the effectiveness of this method in representing complex datasets and enhancing user interaction with visualized information.

The article explores the concept of geometric invariance in computer vision, covering foundational theories such as algebraic invariants and their applications in object recognition, model matching, and 3D imaging. It includes contributions from various researchers on topics like projective geometry, noise-resistant curves, and the use of moment-based recognition techniques.

The article presents a fuzzy neural network model based on multilayer perceptron architecture that utilizes a backpropagation algorithm for pattern classification, incorporating membership values to linguistic properties as input. It highlights the model's effectiveness in handling uncertainty during training and demonstrates its performance in speech recognition, comparing results with conventional MLP and other classifiers.
