While the parent paper addresses the broader cognitive aspects of visual number discrimination, the child paper extends this knowledge by surveying and analyzing deep learning approaches to salient object detection, thereby inheriting and expanding upon the theoretical framework established in the parent work.

In 1970, article The range and scope of binocular depth discrimination in man was published and referenced article Precision and Validity of Stereoscopic Depth Perception from Double Images* that was published in 1953

The child paper builds on the findings of the parent paper by exploring the range and scope of binocular depth discrimination, specifically focusing on disparity cues and their effects on stereoacuity. It extends the parent paper's discussion by examining the relationship between depth perception and neurophysiology, while also addressing the performance of depth discrimination in various spatial positions.
In 1953, article The number of farthest points was published and referenced article The number of farthest points that was published in 1953

The child paper directly references the parent paper, indicating that it builds on the foundational concepts presented in the parent work regarding the number of farthest points in metric spaces. Both papers share similar content and terminology, suggesting that the child paper extends the theoretical framework established by the parent paper.
In 1954, article The convolution transform was published and referenced article The convolution transform that was published in 1954

The child paper builds on the concepts introduced in the parent paper, specifically focusing on the application of the convolution transform in various fields such as image analysis and machine learning. Both papers discuss the convolution operation, but the child paper likely extends the parent paper's foundational work by applying it to contemporary topics in computer vision and deep learning.

In 1968, article Model for Visual Luminance Discrimination and Flicker Detection was published and referenced article Critical Duration in Visual Brightness Discrimination for Retinal Areas of Various Sizes that was published in 1955

The child paper builds on the parent paper by proposing a model for visual luminance discrimination and flicker detection that incorporates findings related to visual perception and brightness discrimination from the parent study. It extends the understanding of visual processing by introducing a new framework that accounts for individual differences and neural connectivity, while also predicting psychophysical results relevant to flicker detection.

In 1964, article Recognition of Colored Road Traffic Light Signals by Normal and Color-Vision-Defective Observers* was published and referenced article Recognition of Red and Green Point Sources by Color-Deficient Observers* that was published in 1955

The child paper builds on the parent paper by investigating the recognition of colored road traffic light signals specifically for normal and color-vision-defective observers, extending the findings of color discrimination under different conditions. It also employs similar statistical techniques to analyze reaction times and errors, suggesting a deeper exploration of the implications for traffic signal standards based on the parent paper's foundational work on color-deficient observers.

In 1975, article Review and Analysis of Color Coding Research for Visual Displays was published and referenced article Recognition of Red and Green Point Sources by Color-Deficient Observers* that was published in 1955

The child paper builds on the parent paper by reviewing and analyzing the effects of color coding in visual displays, which includes considerations of color perception relevant to color-deficient observers as discussed in the parent paper. While the parent paper focuses on the specific recognition capabilities of color-deficient individuals, the child paper extends this knowledge by synthesizing broader experimental findings related to color visual search and identification performance.
In 1970, article The range and scope of binocular depth discrimination in man was published and referenced article Qualitative Depth Localization with Diplopic Images that was published in 1956

The child paper builds on the parent paper by expanding the investigation of depth discrimination using disparity cues, specifically focusing on the limits of binocular depth perception and its neurophysiological implications. While the parent paper establishes foundational findings on qualitative depth localization with diplopic images, the child paper further explores the spatial dynamics and thresholds of depth discrimination in a broader context.

In 1955, article Image Gradation, Graininess and Sharpness in Television and Motion-Picture Systems: Part IV, A & B: Image Analysis in Photographic and Television Systems (Definition and Sharpness) was published and referenced article Characteristics of an image-forming system that was published in 1956

The child paper builds on the theoretical framework established in the parent paper by applying the concepts of image formation and analysis to optical electrical image-forming systems, specifically through the formulation of an "aperture theory." It extends the parent paper's discussion by introducing mathematical techniques such as convolution to analyze image characteristics in greater detail.

In 1960, article Statistical Recognition Functions and the Design of Pattern Recognizers was published and referenced article An optimum character recognition system using decision functions that was published in 1957

The child paper builds on the concepts introduced in the parent paper by discussing the design of pattern recognizers and optimization of recognition functions, which are foundational elements in character recognition systems. It extends the parent paper's focus on decision functions by providing a specific model for categorization and recognition in pattern recognition tasks.
In 1961, article An Analog Method for Character Recognition was published and referenced article An optimum character recognition system using decision functions that was published in 1957

The child paper builds on the parent paper by proposing an analog method for character recognition that utilizes concepts from statistical decision theory, specifically focusing on maximizing cross-correlation for improved discriminability. While the parent paper discusses an optimum character recognition system using decision functions, the child paper extends this work by exploring a different implementation approach that may enhance performance in practical applications.
In 1968, article State of the art in pattern recognition was published and referenced article An optimum character recognition system using decision functions that was published in 1957

The child paper reviews various techniques in pattern recognition, including those relevant to character recognition, and builds on the foundational concepts presented in the parent paper regarding statistical decision theory and optimization in character recognition systems. While it does not directly compare experimental results, it extends the parent paper's work by discussing a broader range of methodologies and applications in the field of pattern recognition.
In 1962, article Head-Mounted Eye-Marker Camera was published and referenced article Eye Fixations Recorded on Changing Visual Scenes by the Television Eye-Marker that was published in 1958

The child paper, "Head-Mounted Eye-Marker Camera," builds on the foundational concepts presented in the parent paper, "Eye Fixations Recorded on Changing Visual Scenes by the Television Eye-Marker," by introducing a novel head-mounted optical device for tracking eye movements in dynamic environments. While the parent paper focuses on television techniques for gaze recording, the child paper extends this work by proposing a wearable technology that enhances the understanding of visual attention in real-time scenarios.
In 1959, article Pattern Detection and Recognition was published and referenced article Experiments in processing pictorial information with a digital computer that was published in 1958

The child paper builds on the parent paper by expanding the discussion of pattern recognition and detection, specifically focusing on the implementation of these concepts in character recognition and pattern detection using a digital computer. It references the foundational ideas presented in the parent paper regarding the processing of pictorial information and automating character sensing.
In 1969, article Picture processing by computer was published and referenced article Experiments in processing pictorial information with a digital computer that was published in 1958

The child paper builds on the foundational concepts presented in the parent paper by expanding the discussion on picture processing techniques and applications, specifically focusing on efficient encoding, approximation methods, and pattern recognition.
